In computer science, a tagged union is a data structure used to hold a value that could take on several different, but fixed, types.[1]

Mathematically, tagged unions correspond to disjoint unions, usually written using +. Given an element of a disjoint union A+B {\displaystyle A+B} , it is possible to determine whether it came from or from the other type.

Description

[edit]

Like option types, tagged unions can be used to handle errors via a monad with the following functions:

f{\displaystyle {\texttt {fmap}}\colon (A\to B)\to \left(\left(A+E\right)\to \left(B+E\right)\right)=f\mapsto a\mapsto {\begin{cases}{\text{err}}\,e&{\text{if}}\ a={\text{err}}\,e\\{\text{value}}\,{\text{(}}\,f\,a'\,{\text{)}}&{\text{if}}\ a={\text{value}}\,a'\end{cases}}}

See also

[edit]
  • Discriminator, the type tag for discriminated unions in CORBA
  • Variant type (COM)

[edit]
  1. ↑ https://arxiv.org/pdf/2111.03354 p. 8
